summary: This cluster investigates the use of low-diversity algal systems, particularly monocultures, for biodiesel production within open reactor systems fed by wastewater. A key finding was that monocultures produced significantly higher concentrations of fatty acid methyl ester (FAME) than diverse algal assemblages, with over 80% of lipids successfully converted to FAME. The research demonstrated effective wastewater bioremediation, as the algae reduced temperature, dissolved solids, pH, and dissolved oxygen while removing nitrate and phosphorus over a 14-week experimental period. The geographic and ecological scope is broadly applicable to cost-effective, wastewater-fed biofuel systems, highlighting a viable pathway for integrating nutrient removal with renewable energy production.
